title: The Scavenger
desc: Useful things grow among the roots. So do useless things. And worse things.

steps.text:
The Wooden Pillar's root system extends deep into the surrounding stone, and where roots meet moisture, things grow. Useful things — resins, fibers, medicinal lichens. Useless things — slime molds, fungal blooms, colonies of cave-beetles. And sometimes, worse things.

A scavenger is picking through the root zone with practiced hands. She knows every species, every growth pattern, every warning sign. "Stick with me. Take what I take. Leave what I leave. And if I run, you run. Don't ask why — just run."

The root zone smells of damp earth and something sweetly rotten.

options.label: Follow her lead — take what she takes

steps.text:
The scavenger works fast. Resin — she scores a root with a knife and collects the amber sap in a jar. Fibers — she peels thin strips from the outer bark, coiling them for rope-makers. Lichens — she scrapes patches of blue-green growth into a cloth pouch.

"The resin sells to smiths — it's a natural flux. The fiber is stronger than hemp. The lichen is medicine." She's talking and working simultaneously, never pausing. "Everything here is useful if you know what it is."

You collect behind her, filling your bag. The root zone is eerie but productive. Occasionally something moves in the shadows — cave-beetles, probably. The scavenger ignores them.

After two hours, your bags are full. She counts the haul and nods. "Good run. No complications." She says 'no complications' like someone who's had many.

You head back to the market with enough raw materials to trade for a week's expenses.

options.label: Sell the haul and split the profits

steps.text:
"Mold-blooms." She says it flatly. "Some of the roots host a fungus that blooms without warning. Releases spores that do things to your head. You breathe them in and you see the root zone differently. Better. More colorful. More alive. You want to stay. You want to lie down among the roots and sleep."

She taps a scar on her forearm. "I slept for sixteen hours once. They found me curled up against a root, smiling. Took two days to stop seeing colors."

You give the roots more space after that.

The scavenging goes well — resin, fiber, lichen. No mold-blooms. The scavenger relaxes as you work deeper, filling bags with materials. The root zone is productive and, today, safe.

As you leave, she points at a cluster of purple-gray growths on a distant root. "Those are new. Those weren't here last week." She stares at them.

"Probably nothing." She doesn't go closer. You don't either.

options.label: Leave with full bags and unanswered questions
